evals.expected_output: Should identify SQL injection (blocker), SRP violation, bare except, missing transaction, N+1 query potential, and magic number 0.1. Should use severity levels and provide concrete fix suggestions.
assertions: Identifies SQL injection as a blocker (string interpolation in SQL)
Flags SRP violation (validate + calculate + save + email in one function)
Catches the bare except clause
Notes the magic number 0.1 (discount percentage)
Suggests parameterized queries
Uses severity levels (blocker/suggestion/nit)
Provides concrete code suggestions for fixes
Notes something positive (if applicable)

evals.prompt: Can you review the changes in my last git commit?
evals.expected_output: Should read the git diff, understand context from surrounding files, and perform a structured review.
assertions: Runs git diff or git show to read the changes
Reads surrounding code for context

For any new helper, export, or block, greps the repo beyond the diff for an existing twin, for importers, and for consumers (duplication / dead export / unconsumed surface are invisible in a single hunk)
Follows the structured review format (summary, blockers, suggestions, nits)
Provides specific file:line references for findings

evals.expected_output: Should flag DRY violation (three nearly identical functions), Promise<any> types, and suggest a more flexible approach (e.g., include options parameter). Should be constructive and suggest a concrete refactoring.
assertions: Flags DRY violation across the three functions
Flags the use of any return type
Suggests consolidation with an options/includes parameter
Does not just say 'this is bad' — provides a refactored version
Notes that this might be a case for a single flexible query function
Applies KISS — suggested solution is not over-engineered

pressure_tests.expected_behavior: Flags that two of the three tests re-verify zlib's own behavior rather than the project's logic: asserting the gzip magic bytes and that compression shrinks the payload are zlib's contract, covered by zlib's own tests, and add maintenance cost without testing our code. Recommends keeping the round-trip and adding tests for OUR logic (the legacy-fallback branches), not the library's.
assertions: Flags the 'gzip magic bytes' test as re-verifying third-party (zlib) behavior
Flags the 'shrinks a big payload' test as testing zlib's compression, not our code
Does NOT praise the file as good coverage just because it has more tests
Keeps/endorses the round-trip test as legitimate own-logic coverage
Suggests testing our actual logic instead (e.g. the legacy fallback branches)

pressure_tests.expected_behavior: Flags the except branch as a fail-open correctness bug, not graceful degradation: on a parse failure the function returns passed=True / no issues, so an unparseable model reply is silently converted into 'everything checks out' and the article publishes unchecked. Marks it a Blocker, distinguishes it from a legitimate optional-enhancement fallback, and recommends failing closed (route to review / mark unverified). Does not accept 'degrades gracefully' as justification.
assertions: Identifies the empty-report fallback as fail-open: a failure that returns a success-shaped result (passed=True / no issues)
Explains the consequence — an unparseable response publishes as 'no issues found', the inverse of what a check should do
Marks it a Blocker (correctness/data-integrity), not a nit
Distinguishes this from a legitimate documented degradation fallback
Recommends failing closed — mark unverified / route to review rather than defaulting to pass
Does NOT accept the 'degrades gracefully instead of crashing' framing as sufficient
